one_line_takeaway: A four-role 'mock court' of LLM agents (prosecutor / defense / judge / jury) roughly doubles pairwise vulnerability-detection accuracy over single- and two-agent baselines on PrimeVul, and — with just the judge agent lightly instruction-tuned on 50 pairs — reaches P-C=96/435 while finding real zero-days including CVE-2025-49000.
key_finding: With GPT-4o and no tuning, VulTrial nearly doubles the best baselines' P-C (81 vs 40 for CoT single-agent and 44 for GPTLens; 2.03x and 1.84x respectively), and is roughly 12x the best DL baseline LineVul (P-C=7). Instruction-tuning ONLY the moderator agent on 50 pairs is the single biggest lever, pushing P-C to 96 (+15 over non-tuned VulTrial, +56 over single-agent, +52 over multi-agent) — while tuning the security-researcher or code-author agents actually hurt (P-C dropped to 54 and 52) by making them over-refute. A single debate round is best (P-C 96 > 87 at 2 rounds > 81 at 3); more rounds let the code author erode valid findings. Ablation confirms every agent matters: removing the moderator collapses P-C from 96 to 32, and removing both moderator+review board drops it to 9 (nearly everything mislabeled vulnerable, P-V=407). VulTrial generalizes to LLaMA-3.1-8B (P-C=89, beating GPTLens 80 and CoT 4). In the wild it found real zero-days with zero false positives on the 3 repos, including CVE-2025-49000 (an unbounded 'skip' parameter DoS in InvenTree's label_sheet plugin) and two confirmed CWE-400 resource-exhaustion functions in DataHub.
core_contribution: The paper proposes VulTrial, a courtroom-inspired multi-agent LLM framework for detecting vulnerabilities in source code AND producing natural-language explanations for each verdict. Four role-specific agents run sequentially over the same base LLM (prompt-induced roles, not different models): a security researcher (prosecutor) that identifies candidate vulnerabilities with reasoning and impact; a code author (defense attorney) that refutes or acknowledges+mitigates them; a moderator (judge) that impartially summarizes both sides; and a review board (jury) that renders the final per-vulnerability verdict (valid / partially valid / invalid), assigns severity (low/medium/high/none), and a recommended action. A function is flagged vulnerable only if at least one raised vulnerability is judged valid at high severity with action 'fix immediately' — a deliberately conservative threshold to cut false positives. Built on the AgentVerse framework. Two secondary contributions: (1) role-specific instruction tuning of individual agents using only 50 validation code pairs, and (2) an in-the-wild deployment that surfaced confirmed zero-day vulnerabilities. This is the paper's OWN system; GPTLens, CodeBERT, LineVul, PrimeVul, AgentVerse, etc. are cited/baseline work, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: This is a defensive vulnerability-detection tool, so the relevant 'threat' is the class of software vulnerabilities in C/C++ (and, in the wild, Python) source code — subtle, non-obvious vulnerable code changes where benign and vulnerable functions share >=80% of their string content, making them hard to distinguish. The paper does not analyze adversarial attacks against the detector itself (e.g., prompt injection or evasion of the LLM agents); its scope is detection accuracy and explanation quality, plus real-world discovery of previously-unknown vulnerabilities.
limitations: Higher false-positive rate is the main cost of higher recall: VulTrial GPT-4o FPR=0.52 vs CoT's 0.17 (though below GPTLens's 0.62). Evaluated on essentially ONE benchmark (PrimeVul pairs, C/C++ only); authors avoid other datasets over bias concerns but this limits external validity. All agents share a single underlying model, relying on prompt-induced role diversity rather than genuinely different models. Instruction tuning is brittle — tuning multiple agents together introduces over-alignment (the fine-tuned review board learns to 'follow' an assertive fine-tuned code author, raising P-B to 331 when all agents are tuned). Multi-round debate degrades accuracy rather than improving it. Cost scales with model: full VulTrial on GPT-4o is ~$32 for the test set (vs $8.10 GPT-4o single-agent), though VulTrial on cheap GPT-3.5 ($7.46) still beats GPT-4o single-agent. In-the-wild validation is a small pilot (3 repos, 218 functions total) with a conservative reporting policy, so it measures precision-of-disclosure, not recall in the wild. Data-leakage risk from undisclosed GPT training data is only partially mitigated (via LLaMA cutoff and the zero-day findings).

datasets_used: PrimeVul paired dataset (Ding et al.): 3,789 train / 480 validation / 435 test pairs; vulnerable and benign functions share >=80% string content (primary benchmark)
Full PrimeVul dataset: 175,797 training functions (4,862 vulnerable / 170,935 benign) and 23,948 validation functions (593 vulnerable / 23,355 benign) — used to fine-tune DL baselines
In-the-wild repositories: Apache Superset (47 functions analyzed), DataHub (160 functions), InvenTree (11 functions), selected from 196 GitHub repos with a SECURITY.md and >1,000 stars
quantitative_results: GPT-4o, no tuning: VulTrial P-C=81 vs CoT single-agent P-C=40 (2.03x) and GPTLens P-C=44 (1.84x); VulTrial P=0.53, R=0.60, FPR=0.52
GPT-3.5: VulTrial P-C=68 vs CoT P-C=18 (3.8x) and GPTLens P-C=20 (3.4x), FPR=0.23
Best DL baseline LineVul P-C=7 (FPR=0.21, R=0.22); VulTrial GPT-3.5 P-C=68 is ~10x, GPT-4o P-C=81 is ~12x
Instruction-tuning moderator only (GPT-4o): P-C=96, +15 over non-tuned (81), +56 over single-agent, +52 over multi-agent baseline
Instruction-tuning other agents individually hurt: security researcher-tuned P-C=54, code author-tuned P-C=52, review board-tuned P-C=72, all-four-tuned P-C=45
Debate rounds (moderator-tuned GPT-4o): 1 round P-C=96, 2 rounds P-C=87, 3 rounds P-C=81
Ablation (moderator-tuned GPT-4o base P-C=96): no moderator P-C=32, no code author P-C=63, no moderator+code author P-C=63, no moderator+review board P-C=9 (P-V=407)
LLaMA-3.1-8B: VulTrial P-C=89 (P=0.52, R=0.48, FPR=0.45) vs GPTLens P-C=80 and CoT P-C=4
Explanation quality (Actionability, CoT vs synthesized VulTrial): Claude-Haiku-4.5 judge 2.15 vs 4.00; human annotators 2.37 vs 4.52 (VulTrial higher on all four dimensions in both evaluations)
In the wild: DataHub 2 alarms / 2 true positives, InvenTree 1 alarm / 1 true positive (CVE-2025-49000), Superset 0 alarms; zero false positives
Cost: GPT-3.5 VulTrial $7.46 vs GPT-4o single-agent $8.10 vs GPT-4o VulTrial ~$32; instruction tuning ~$17/agent (50 pairs) vs PrimeVul-style tuning >$500 (>15,000 samples)
baselines_compared: CodeBERT (DL single-agent, fine-tuned on full and paired PrimeVul)
LineVul (DL single-agent, line-level; best-performing DL baseline)
Ding et al.'s Chain-of-Thought prompting (LLM single-agent baseline from PrimeVul)
GPTLens (two-agent auditor+critic multi-agent baseline, adapted from smart contracts)
Base LLMs evaluated: GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-0125), GPT-4o (gpt-4o-2024-08-06), and LLaMA-3.1-8B
